Case presentation: A 53-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of cough and progressively worsening difficulty walking up the stairs in his apartment. He has noticed that it is easier for him to climb the stairs after he has exercised. He has also had a 4.6-kg (10.1-lb) weight loss over the past 6 months. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 35 years. Examination shows dry mucous membranes. The pupils are equal and react sluggishly to light. Muscle strength in the proximal lower extremity is initially 3/5 but increases to 5/5 after repeated muscle tapping. His achilles reflex is 1+. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
Early Candidate Rationale: The early symptoms of difficulty walking up stairs and cough could suggest Lambert-Eaton syndrome due to muscle weakness, which is often proximal and can affect activities like climbing stairs. Dermatomyositis is also considered due to muscle weakness, but the absence of skin symptoms makes it less likely. Polymyalgia rheumatica is less likely due to the age and symptom profile.
Revision Summary: The final choice changed to Lambert-Eaton syndrome due to the characteristic improvement in muscle strength after exercise and the association with smoking history.
Revision Evidence: The full case details, such as improvement in muscle strength after exercise and the patient's smoking history, strongly suggest Lambert-Eaton syndrome, which is often associated with paraneoplastic syndromes like small cell lung cancer. The initial ranking of Dermatomyositis and Polymyalgia rheumatica was contradicted by these specific symptoms and history.
